speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Willa Cummings Carlson was recorded at her home in Moscow on January 15th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I don't know when the Chautauquas came in to Troy. I don't know. But I went after I got to going to school in high school to try and I don't think they'd been there before. They talked about the Chautauquas. I think it was brand new when the Chautauquas came around. But I didn't say what it was. But it was in the early 1900s. In 1900s. I don't know it was that early. I never got away from home to go to anything before 1910.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And they didn't have them then. No, wait a minute. I'm getting ahead of myself. I went to Lewiston to school and went to summer school in Lewiston. That's where Chautauquas were intended. And then after that, there were Chautauquas in Troy. I don't believe they did come to small towns before that. I think they'd hit Lewiston and Moscow. I don't know about Moscow. I don't know about Moscow. Excuse me. But I went to them in Lewiston and I thought, my, how wonderful they were. And they gave the students at the normal school a special rate for a ticket. And it just so happened that I had a chance to help a woman with two card prizes with the refreshments. And I got a little, I didn't want this to put in. Don't let that go on record. But I got money to go. I think it was only a dollar and a half we got a season ticket. And they had three or four days. And there were all kinds of speeches. There were speeches for educational reasons and then there were speeches for inspiration. And then of course they were discussed at the normal by the teachers over there. And they had pretty good teachers at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Discussed after what?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: After we had heard them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Discussed in class, huh? In the classes?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well they wouldn't discuss them very much in class as a lesson. But they would mention them and ask us about the highlights and what we liked about them. I can't remember much about them to be honest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was the Chautauqua set up? Was it intense?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Intense. In a big Chautauqua tent. And they had their people come and I don't know where they stayed. I wasn't looking after the machinery of anything at that time. But I think they stayed in the hotels. And they went right on to the next place you know. They weren't travelling in cars then from place to place. Not right then, not the first time. First time I went to law school was 1914 or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So on the whole, what were they like?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: There was music. There was a varied program of lectures and music. And the music I remember the first time I ever heard the Swiss Yodelers. There was a group of Swiss Yodelers came and there was a little background sketch. And those sketches I loved because they told us something that most of us were starved to hear. About how these people lived. And then there were Scotch Highlanders and they came you know in their... There were Scotch Highlanders. They came in their kilts and the bagpipes. That's where I first heard the bagpipes was Chautauqua.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And they had, for music that's what they had. And then they had a symphony orchestra. And if I had been a little bit more musically inclined or been let's say a little bit more musically talented. I think I'm going to have to teach one of the non -darian ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they were all a part of the same show?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: All part of the... Oh it was a program and you subscribed to the Chautauqua. And the Chautauqua people had their program planned and they came one after another. On Monday you'd have certain things. On Tuesday and Wednesday there were three days of it I remember. And you took the regular program and they went on from now from Lewiston. Well they went on to the next place. Went on down the river I suppose. I don't know where they went. But eventually they wound up at Portland I suppose. And but Idaho... I don't know. They might have gone off to Salt Lake. Because Utah has long been on that program. I don't know at that time. I wasn't so interested. I was interested in getting to the Lewiston Normal and getting some credits so I could get to teaching. And I was interested in that and very young.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And more than young I was very inexperienced. I didn't know how to find out things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well that was your first time away from American Ridge wasn't it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I mean to not to be live at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of? What was it like to be living in Lewiston on your own and as a young person?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: It certainly wouldn't be typical if I told you what I went through. It would have been very untypical. I went down there and I found out that my folks could not pay me. Could not pay for my board and they told me that. And my mother wasn't going to allow me to go away from home. She needed me at home. She needed me in the house. She had little children and she didn't see how she was going to be able to do it. And she didn't want me to go. And Papa said well we don't have the money. But if you have found a way to go down there and pay for your board and go to school. He said you can go. I won't stop you. I said I won't say you can't go. And he said my mother and I don't want you to either. That's how I got to go. If I listened to my mother I would have been on American Ridge yet digging away. Possibly covering up the graves and things like that carrying for them. She had no ambition for me. She had personal ambition but my mother did not have ambition for me. But that's neither here nor there and that's not to go in any place. That's not to be on record. Sorry I haven't brought such a thing up. Well I think that one of the things that I've been trying to ask people about more lately is what family life was like and how parents treated their kids. Often I get the idea that kids were not considered important like they are these days. They didn't come first it seems. Well they didn't come first and the privileges they extended to them. But they were regarded as another means of... The workforce. Yes they were the labor force. They couldn't hire labor and so here was a labor force. I remember that Papa was very disgusted. Now the child next to me was a boy. He was four years younger than I and that was wonderful. There was a son and of course he was spoiled as bad as could be and he was given many privileges as a child. Even as Papa he gave him privileges although not as many as my mother extended to him. And then after that there were three girls and every one of them was a keen disappointment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the advantage of having a boy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Feel labor. Farm labor. And it still was frowned upon to put girls out to doing too much of it. And not many girls went out and worked with horses and machinery. Not many. Some did. I didn't. I was a coward. I was afraid of horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think you could have done it if you'd been willing to do it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I might have. I might have worked with the horses. If I'd have been willing, yes. I sort of suspicion I would. I didn't want to. But I would not milk a cow.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I'd made up my mind I would not milk a cow. So I got a metal block. I'd squeeze on the tits and nothing would come. Never did come for me. Never did. I never made any milk come. Sounds like you didn't fancy yourself too much of a manual worker. Well, maybe I had a little hand.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I didn't realize it then. I didn't think of myself as small, but I really was small. I had small hands. I was small. I was fat. I got fat. But I ate too much. Too much is the wrong thing. We certainly had lots of rich food. That's one thing we had plenty of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you eat at home? What did the family eat when you were a kid?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: We ate everything. We ate the finest garden in the world. I've never seen a better home garden than what we had. We had a home orchard. We had all kinds of fruit. We dried the fruit and we canned fruit all summer. It wasn't very long before I was too large when they learned how to can vegetables. At first they didn't know how to can vegetables. I remember with my mother canned corn with a compound she bought. This wasn't really a pinkum. It was a compound of some sort she bought. They were all a little bit afraid to eat it. For fear it might be poisonous. Or it might contaminate the food some way. But it didn't. We ate it as I remember it. I lived to a good old age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they dry corn then?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, we dried corn. I like dried corn and to this day dried corn is much more tasty to me than canned corn. It is me too that I haven't had it for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any special setup that it used for drying corn there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, not particularly. We were particularly lucky. We had a home dryer for prunes. It was a little shed like building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I suppose how many feet would it be?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: About six by six I imagine. I think it was about six by six. Then possibly eight feet tall. Then a roof over it and raised on this elevated underneath it so there was air in there. In the middle of that thing was an old heating stove. Pot bellied, but it was an iron stove. Popo didn't have anything else because he wanted safety. He didn't want to burn down. It was up through the orchard and up through this dryer. I romanticized it. I romanticize it now in my memory. But it was very unromantic to look upon. It wasn't as romantic as it might have been to me even at the time because some things did grow up under it. It wasn't a very noxious weed. Popo didn't mind so much. But any noxious weeds got you working around there, we had to clean them out, snakes or no snakes. But otherwise there were other green things that grew up under there. It looked kind of nice. It looked like carbon under there. I can imagine all sorts of things.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Frogs, I remember there was usually a frog under there. You like to chase him out and hear him holler. But snakes harbored there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right underneath the dryer?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yeah, right underneath there. It was up on legs. I should have said that too. At each corner there was a post. We called them legs, sitting up on legs. They could have been easily pulled out. I don't know, they sold that dryer to somebody so somebody did come eventually.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there wasn't a whole floor?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: There was a floor up above that. There was a floor and then the posts were just at the corner and they went down. Oh yes, it was well built. Nothing on my father's farm was not well built. The chicken house was wonderfully built. Neighbor used to come in to see how it was built. No, nothing was built sloppily. Not there. He wasn't like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In those days, did you dry prunes and you dried corn? Did you dry anything else? Did you dry apples?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yes, we dried apples. But the apples didn't dry in there very nicely. Mama did put them in there but she didn't like them when they dried. I can't remember what the difference was. But they don't dry in the same process as prunes do. But prunes, you know, you dipped them in lye water and went through a routine. It was no small job to dry prunes. And that was bad enough. Dipping them in lye water, I never had to do that. I had to make a cider. Ronnie was asking about cider the other day. He wanted to get a cider press. And I laughed and said, I wonder what became of our old cider press. And he was horrified to learn we never had a cider press. And I said, we certainly did. I didn't care to have it. We made it. And not for the making of cider. Of course we drank a little cider when we first got it made. It was fresh. And brought the apples down there and had all the washed tubs out. And I think we borrowed the washed tub extra. We had to pick up the apples, shake the trees. These pop -up come around, shake the trees. I wasn't big enough to shake them.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And get that green bit of milk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you want some of that in?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: A spoonful. Make this more tasty for me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he would shake the apples off the crayons?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Shake the apples and my brother and I picked them up. Bucket after bucket and put them in the... in tubs. And then we drew, I drew the water out of the cistern. My brother wasn't big enough and strong enough yet to draw the water up. I was four years older. I drew the water out of the well. And we carried it out there and poured it over the apples. We were supposed to wash them. We had rooms, got a new room. And we shook them around. I can imagine it wasn't done too well. But oh, it probably looked on if it was done right. Do that some more. You showed us how. Maybe I did. I got pretty particular about the cider. And of course, you know, we had to pick out the leaves and throw them out. And then we put them through the grinder. A larval like to grind, so he drowned a lot. He did just about as much. Or he got tired of it. Whenever he got tired of it, you do it. He was like that. And he did pretty well for a little candle, I think. We should hang it back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the grinding, that was the main part, wasn't it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know. We ground it and it went down into the baskets. And they were made of cleats, probably called them. I don't know what they were. Like lads, pieces like lads, a little thicker than lads. And very smooth. They'd been planes and everything. They were very smooth.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And then they were wired from the top. I'm afraid my coffee's going to spill. About so high, you see. Oh, this is Ohno, that wasn't the high. This is about the high end. And then so far from the top, there'd be a wire that went around and passed over them and was twisted and went on to another one. And then there was a crack left between them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You get the idea?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Of these lads all around, four walls of them, with the cracks in between. And then they were leveled off. So at the top and bottom were leveled off. And there was a lid that went on the top. And of course the bottom was left at the bottom. And that fitted into the bottom of this press that held them. And then also held a rigging that had the grinder on the, or the mill on the side of the side of the mill. And at the top there was a hopper that we had to put the apples into to grind them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The ground apples then?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: We put the plain apples in there. In the hopper to grind them. And then they ground and went down into the stuff. And the juice squeezed out all around in one of those cleats. And we were supposed to grind that until we got it down to not very much trash lift at the bottom of it. And then of course I don't remember cleaning that out. We had to clean that out.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I don't know. Seems to me we had to have the help of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you make very much cider?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh yes, we had to make two barrels full at least. And then a lot of the neighbors would come in and they'd like to have a gallon or two made. And I just rebelled against that making for the new neighbors. Crazy things. Then not everybody had a cider press at the time. Nobody had a cider press. Nobody had a cider press near us. But our cider was made for vinegar. Well that's what it was made for. And when it got to be vinegar then the neighbors all came with their gallon buckets to get a gallon of vinegar. And jarred. And all the new neighbors got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father sell it to them?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I don't think he ever got a scent. My father fed more people than any man on the American Ridge. And I just got up and fight. Anytime that I hear about how much the church did for people out there. Because the church was good and I loved the church for people. I liked them and they were good to me and they did lots for me. So I never say anything against them except in so far as saying that the church did so much for people who lived out there. And they let it pop out because he never had anything to do with the church. He himself never did. He never went to church. But he saw to it that if I wanted to go I went. I was to go. I was to be free to go because I walked over a mile. The church was back. He didn't hitch up and take me or anything like that until after I was married. And went home to visit and then did I want to go to church and then I could have anything I wanted. And it didn't matter any longer. But that's the way things go. But anyway. He never interfered in my going to church. But somebody starving in the community. So they went around to collect food. And the folks gave.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: They always butchered. We grew up on pork. And he butchered the average of eight hogs all together in the winter. And then maybe a pig or two as you call them in the fall. Before they could. Before they got cold enough to keep meat. And they had for you know for use. Payable news. And so besides that we consumed I think of quantity as a pork. But you say this the family that was that was starving. He'd give meat. They'd get a ham and then they'd get a great big. Yeah. My mother oh dear how much large he had to render. My job that was that was terrible. They rendered it on top of the stove and inside the stove. All that grease and stuff. And then there were so many cracklings left over. She used to grind up the cracklings. We kids would grind up the cracklings. And she'd make soap out even out of the cracklings. That weren't burned. But they didn't make such good soap. But he'd kill a calf a year. He'd even eat meat himself. But my mother liked it and they'd give the neighbors some. And after a while not during that in the first years. But after a while I learned excuse me can the beef. And that was very good meat. My vet made good meat and we can eat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the lard that she rendered mostly used for soap?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: No the lard was used for cooking. Oh for cooking. We weren't like the Chinese or like the Jews we were used for. That was used. So I'm defiled. I'm defiled because I certainly grew up on the lard. Tell me I have a necktie on your shoes. You're a shoes. Sure I don't know if they'll pitch you. Well I have a shoe. But my feet are kind of big and I think. Laura has a pair of two. We just got them in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: They're lying. Well I'll tell you what. Back to vinegar. Now when they. Everybody knew we made that cider and we started what we called the old house. Didn't call it smokehouse like other people did. And that old house I said Papa was very particular about everything he had there. Well he did paint it on the outside to make it look a little bit better. But he bought 40 acres. Well actually 80 acres mostly for pasture and there was a field on top. I'd only got about 15 acres of good land and then an old house on it. And they brought the old house over. All the neighbors came in with their horses and so on. Pulled it over Chris that's nothing to tell about. It was quite a thing. The whole day's proceeding. It sounds like a neighborhood. We're pulling together to get something done. It was. It was a community thing. And Papa was very popular with his neighbors and they were always glad to help him out because he always helped them out with everything they did. And he helped him in more ways than they always appreciated. But anyway. They came and helped him with their horses. I don't have any horses yet. I said 16 I think. And they bought that old house over and stood it in the yard. And that white house out there on the other side of the well you know. Did they have a still called it the old house. Did they have a party to go along with that. Did your mother give them a feed or something like that to go. Yes I think they did. They came in the house as Nate. They didn't come and sit down so I think it was around the table and so on. And I don't suppose I was in there. That's why I don't know the kids. We were all out around singing. But I'm sure they did because they all ate. And it was it was a pretty major undertaking. I mean it took a matter of hours. Took the whole afternoon. I think the I don't believe they came until noon or afternoon. But they did have lunch.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And I don't remember partaking of the lunch. We kids got in the way you know. We would have been shut it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that's where you stored things in that room?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yeah that's where we had things stored. People had smoke houses to store things in. We had a little bit of a house. Didn't have room to store anything. Popo stored a lot of stuff he had no business to store in that house. I hated it. Never could make his front room look like anything because he always had seed corn or something drying in back of the stove. And things like that because that was important to him. That was next year's crop. It wasn't important to me. And the thing that was important to me was the house looked like something. And I remember how I hated that stuff. But that's a good thing he did store vinegar in there. But that he couldn't have stood the smell himself so that was it. Well he locked it up in there in the old house because. And he'd send me out after onions and I'd have to take the key in and lock the house after it got to be vinegar. Because he'd heard the people had said they were going to come in while it was hard cider. They'd come in and have a party. And they did sometimes. They'd get in and steal. The hard cider. And I don't know I was stealing vinegar. I don't remember that anybody did that. They'd come and ask and get the vinegar. But the hard cider. Oh they said oh Charlie that was a glorious drunk.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And I don't doubt it was. But they didn't dare get drunk on the place. Papa said I'll take the shotgun. I've got a shotgun that's loaded. I guess they were crazy. So they teased him about it anyway. Oh yes. They took some too. I remember there aren't you may or it's instrumental. You know this this family that was that was having a hard time and that sounds like. A measure of what of what charity was like in those days. What was their charity like that going on or family having charity. There weren't very many. But now another poor family moved into the neighborhood and there wasn't a great deal of sympathy. And expanded upon them if they weren't. To a Syrian woman. And she couldn't even speak English but she had come to visit her brother who lived there. He was a. He managed to make himself quite an American and.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And took his place among citizens. And it was all right. I remember the man. Very dark and I never knew there was anything the matter with him. I use anything but like all other people I was terribly dark. But his sister came to visit him and she married this old man. And they said that he bought her from where you know stories went out. I don't think this was true but this story did go out. And he bought her from her brother. But anyway they had a whole mess of children. I don't know. Must have had five or six. And all little youngsters and they moved out into a house. In the country. Well he didn't work. He was sat around the house. He was an old man. She married him. And one winter he was sick. She said he was sick. Well I don't know. They got a doctor for him and he got supposed to have gotten better. And somebody got a job for him down Troy. And they said he could stay as brother in law. Them of his. Providing him with a shed or some place to stay in and make that even. Got him a job so he could work at it. And do something for the family. And then the people out there took food in. They came around collecting some. Buddy with a. Spring wagon they called him. A little wagon they have little cars and big cars you know little pickups. A pickup in a truck. Well they had a little a little wagon and a big wagon at that time. But no he switched the big wagon on the road I remember. Some women I think came around and picked it up. Picked up food for family. Picked up food and Papa gave a couple sacks of potatoes and sack of cabbage and. A lot of squashes and crunches and. Oh all the winter vegetables.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this the same family that you mentioned before that was starving?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well I don't know what I told about that. Oh well no just a little while ago when you said that when you said church didn't help so much. Oh well I called him a starving family. I don't think anybody would have let them starve. Nobody would have been carrying and meals by the meal before they would have starved in that community. Bread and things like that but. I don't think Papa did give flour because other people were giving flour and things like that. And they ground wheat and they took ground wheat for a cereal you know. That's nothing better than that on Black Castle. I said I love it. And I fed my children on it. And I had a good neighbor too. I had a mighty good neighbor that ground wheat for me and gave it to me.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And I had a good neighbor that was a good neighbor. It was nice either but our neighbor had a grinder and he ground the wheat. But it was quite a job because they had to wash the wheat you know and grind it and that hand grinder. And my goodness it was good. I'd forgotten what they called that hand grinder. They had kind of a name for it but it was a little meal. And they ground it for their own home use but I know that our relatives came to visit and they thought it was the best breakfast cereal we'd ever eaten. And it is very good. The whole wheat and it's nutty and my children all loved it. So I had before and they all liked it. When they, Rayland and Lonnie, both had them. We had two friends and had those meals. And those two kept us supplied when we were on American Regents. I remember having it on Burnt Ridge too. I was thinking Uncle Laura did it but I'm not sure. Maybe it was Lonnie. Lonnie. When we were on Burnt Ridge. On Burnt Ridge. That's Lonnie. That's the only kind of hot cereal I could ever stand. And it was good. I didn't even call it Graham. Graham, yes. We did because you can buy Graham far and make it but you know it isn't. You don't buy that kind of stuff. You don't buy the real thing. I wonder if you can get that anymore. Oh I think you can. What made it so good was it just said it was fresh and fresh and fresh. It's whole wheat. It's whole wheat. And then it was, they said it was stone cut. But on those hand mills that there are stones in them to cut, for cutting in some way. It was specially cut. And then you... I think it was less fine than what you get now. And you cooked it a long time. I put it to soak the night before. I think I cooked it some the night before. Then in the morning got up and poured out water on it and let it cook some more. It took a long time where it wasn't soft enough to eat. And then that made sure you were getting the milk you should have too on your cereal. And all that you ever liked it. Never had any trouble having to eat it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that, was that, it sounded like you were saying a little bit rugged living out there when you were just married and had kids? Or was it just what you were used to?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: No. My husband wouldn't. Certainly not like Tammy conveying that impression. No. We were making the transition from Idaho County where we had gone. Absolutely fat broke you might say.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: We didn't accept it that way. I went out to teach school. And my husband proceeded to save from the wreckage. Now I don't like this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This isn't going on that thing is it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well I can turn it off if you want. It was in the early 30s. I was over on American Ridge in 30, I went over there. We had typhoid fever in 1929. That was the year when the Wall Street crash came in 1929. And that's when all went down and everything was down around our ears for a few years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what year was it? 1933 when Roosevelt was elected?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: He went in in 1933 and declared the moratorium. I came back from California in 1933 and you had already been on American Ridge for a year. Yeah. That's right. We had been on American Ridge for a year. But we were still trying to come out of it. And then we lived in the Harlan Hunt. No. You weren't there in 1933. You went down and we had it in 1929. The doctor said that you'd have to go. You should have summer without the cold. That he didn't know about your little legs. And you went down in 1930. You went down in 30 and back in 1931. No. Taking those years off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What had made you decide to leave to go to Idaho County in the first place?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, my husband had a great ambition. A dear, a lifelong dream. That one day he'd own a big cattle ranch. And that he would make his living and have his family. And he loved the kids. But of course he found out that they were more than he, more of a handful than he'd ever thought they would be. Because they had to be cared for. And they did get sick at times and had to have doctors. And we had to have food and we had to have clothing. And he wanted me to teach when it was convenient for him to have me gone. And when he wasn't, he wanted the convenience of me at home taking care of the kids and also me out teaching. And of course he just couldn't have everything. But finally he decided that he couldn't make the payments on the place any longer. And he went over there and decided that this was a good deal. And the man thought he was going broke and he was selling at a sacrifice. Well, he wasn't. He was selling before it hit bottom. And my husband, well, paid the penalty. He never could pay out. He never could pay out. Folks don't said that to him. But he did it with the best of intentions. He certainly never intended that anything like that had happened to us. And he didn't know he was going to be overcome by so many terrible things. And everything, you know, everything seemed to conspire against him. Well, certainly when that, when a depression hit, it seemed to, it did it to so many people. Yeah. And then his coming back to Laitau County, I have always had a feeling. I have a feeling for Laitau County. Laitau County is my county. I love it. I love Moscow. I like to live here. I like Laitau County. I like American Ridge. I like Kendrick, Detroit and all these places. They're my home. And out on American Ridge among the old timers, if you'd have gone out there, you'd have had a hard time finding any words spoken against me. Of course, the younger people might have different ideas, but I don't know about that. So you wanted to come back. You were real happy about coming back. I was already come back. I said, if I go back, they'll understand me and they'll know, they'll know what I mean. And they'll see. I wasn't going to ask them for help. I won't have to ask them for help. I knew that. But I'll just, they'll understand. They'll understand why I do this and why I do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, didn't Daddy have a heart attack in 1929, too?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, yes. He had a heart attack. That's another thing. He was sick before. He had a bad heart when we went over to the war. He'd had a bad heart. He bought the place in 1922. Yes, well, 21. I got in the bargain for it and didn't take me down to see it. And of course, his father always waited for that and said it was partly the fault. I think it was. If I had gone over there and seen that horrible place, I don't think I ever would have gone. I would have said, no, no, let's don't. I don't want to go in on this. But as it was, I said, well, you're going to make the living. Yes, he wasn't going to have me teaching school anymore. That was just out of the question. I was going to stay home and take care of the kids. And he didn't want that. And on a home, I had to live in a rattle. Well, that's neither here nor there. But anyway, it was a kind of a bad side of the dream. Dreams don't always pan out, you know. But yet, there were many good things about that place. I think back to that, the Timbred 40 that we looked out upon, and I think back to the woods down below the place. I used to go there on Sundays with the children. We used to gather in a little spot that Paul called our park, our clover park. The only thing that spoiled it was we had those little minkettled roaming around, and everybody went to bull was coming around to visit us in our area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what would you do there, picnic?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I remember the park, and I loved the park. Oh, I did too. I did too. I loved it. There were many things about that place I loved. I just simply loved. I even loved the spring where we went to get the water. I thought that was beautiful. But it wasn't nice to go down there and carry two heavy buckets of water, which the doctor said contributed to my getting the way I was when I got typhoid. Well, anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This park was a flat area with trees?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: No, it wasn't. It was a little flat spot. I wouldn't call it an area. It was such a small area. But it was a flat spot on this hillside, and it was gently sloping. It gently sloped. It wasn't a real flat place. But it was a gentle slope, and somehow Dutch clover had been seated on the place in some field, and some of it had been spilled or something, or wind rolling or something. So this Dutch clover had come in there and had choked out the native grasses, and mostly it was that clover. And the bees hung around. They got used to us being there. We would never harm a bee in that particular part. None of the children were ever stung there, as I can remember. Later on, Bruce was indenturous and got stung. Always stung by yellow jackets, but not by the bees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your kids would play there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Was that... Oh, my yes. They played in that clover and how they loved it. And I'd go on Sunday afternoons with them. I didn't dare let them go down there alone. I forbade them to do that. And especially let the little ones go. I didn't dare to, on account of the cattle. Well, Bruce and I went all the time. Well, you went with somebody else. Oh, or... Well, you had Keith with us. Keith, my brother Keith had just three years older than me, but he was always considered an adult from birth.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: So if he was taking care of us, that would say we're good hands. He was pretty good at taking care of us, for that's what I was concerned. He always had an idea. He always had some idea about how to do it. Oh, yes. Yes. There was good things about that place. And it was a community. I used to say it seemed God forsaken. The children were hungry and know something about the hereafter. They asked me questions at school. I taught there three and a half years. And I liked the children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The kids wanted to know about spiritual?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yes, they were very much concerned. And I got old stories of the East, Baldur's, old stories of the East, and I read them to them. I read all those stories about Deborah Hunt, Bail, false gods. I explained something to them about gods, false gods, and the one god, Christians.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many of the families struggling that were on Harris Ridge then?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, they were all struggling. They were all struggling, but they were very happy in their struggles. They had houses in the mountains. They didn't have nice crows. They didn't have nice furniture, but nobody else had. So there was no jealousy among them or anything like that. Kind of anybody got sick, they did things. I did all kinds of things, but now I'm getting old, and I'm in Idaho County. Because in a period of time... I was important over in Idaho County. Maybe I liked that. I don't know. You told... I'd never been called upon on the American Ridge. Imagine somebody calling up and saying to me, Somebody is sick, and you come and help take care of them. Well, I did go once in a while. I went one time where a man had died, and they wanted somebody to stay the night with the family. And I said to Papa, well, you don't need... Papa was going to go. Mama couldn't go. She was unable to go places. And I said, no, I'll go. I went to do some of those things, but never to take care of something. But still, I worked for a couple of women when they had babies, and I took care of the babies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On American Ridge?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yes. When I was 16, somewhere I was 16, I took care of a couple of babies. Watched them. Came to bed every day. Verta claimed my sister to be a tall woman, and I told her brother down in the doctor's office here in town once, why I worked for your mother when your sister Verta was born. And I took care of her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, you did?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, you surely didn't. Yes, I did. I said, I was only 16, but I took care of her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that hard when you were to do that at that age? I mean, were you afraid of that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, sure. It was hard. I was afraid I'd do the wrong thing. I was terribly afraid of that, but I did it. And I watched for that family. She had a father and a brother staying there. And then I went to a place named, well, they had a hired man staying at their house. I remember it was there named Frank. Frank and Rachel are the names of the man in life. Elder. And I took care of them. I was 16. And heavens, I had an awful lot to do. Five dollars a week and do everything. Big washings. Take care of the baby.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to cook, too?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yes, yes. Three square meals a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they sick or something at the time?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, she wasn't exactly well. And she was at the baby. But nobody else was sick. But they were farmers and they had to work outside. They had their hands full. I remember I got panicky one Sunday. I thought I was going to have it easy. And she had said, well, we'll just take it easy. Baked a cake yesterday. I'm going to have cake and then I have fruit. And something else. I was going to make a salad. I was great on salads. Even men. And oh, yes, my kids, they look at me. They think I can't cook.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Bruce out here is the worst one of them. He entertained Blake, I remember one time. I was telling him that Mother didn't cook. She didn't know how to cook this one. Didn't know how to cook that. But she did. Well, I seem to have gotten by with it anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, are they good or not?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Anyway, I was considered a good cook at that time. Among the young people, the young women.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I saved my money. I wanted a dress. I wanted a dress like the other girls. I wanted a coat. I had a coat for several years. I had a coat that looked like Sam Scratch. But I didn't get it. I closed my eyes and ears. I must go to Lewiston Normal School. And I went on fifty dollars. That's what I had. Well, I had fifty -two dollars. Now, I've forgotten exactly how I came by the two dollars. But Papa filled it out and I bought my ticket. The two dollars. That's all it cost to go to Lewiston in those days. And I bought my ticket and I had fifty dollars. Wrapped up in a handkerchief and stowed away in some safe place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you saved for that for a long time?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yes. I made part of the money. Five dollars a week working for... Well, four dollars a week for the first place because it's normal. Well, I could go for four dollars a week because of where those clams were. I could go for four dollars a week because I never had any experience working out for anybody else. Lord knows I'd had experience. And I'd experience taking care of kids, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you have experience?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: At home.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And that was strenuous, I assure you. More strenuous than I ever had working for others.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who set the price?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Your mother set the price or the... Well, I was set by her and the woman for whom I was going to work. That's always the only kind of way to go and wage was for that sort of thing. Yes. Well, I was working for four dollars and five dollars. Those of you that had any experience began to get five dollars. Then when I went to these elders, I got the five dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was this you were starting to say about the Sunday you had to cook?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, these people came in. A family. Man and his wife and all, I'd heard about what a wonderful housekeeper she was. And such a wonderful cook. And I'd tasted some of her cakes. She'd taken some of these socials and things, you know. And here they had come and were staying for supper. Oh! Oh, Frank Elder came out and said, I'll help you. He said, but Rachel was asking for supper. I said, I hope you don't mind. I do mind. I said, I have an ocean to run off. I said, tonight for the first time, I have an ocean to run away. I said, I don't want to get dinner for Mrs. Chandler. I said, I'll help you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What can I do?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Get off the garden and dig some new potatoes and then clean them and get in there. Then you get them in there. And bring in some onions, I said. And I don't remember what else, but I do remember telling him to dig some potatoes and get in there. He did me a clean. Helped a little. Stood around like most people would.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: But they had two cute little boys. I think back to them, yes. Frankie. Frankie and Cecil, my names were. They were the cutest little boys. They were about like Harold Hall, about that age. Two little boys and then they'd had a girl, like they said. There was a girl named Hazel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did you stay there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Four weeks, exactly. $20 bill, I think. I was part of that $20 bill, believe me. I didn't break it either. But I had this money from one of the, no, and then I did things for other people.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And, but after I'd had a year at Lewiston, I was a different person.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you get the idea? How did it come about that you decided you wanted to go to Lewiston?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I had made up my mind that I was going to school or die in the attempt. And that if I didn't go to school, that I would die. I would not live out there on a farm and that I would leave home. I had made up my mind to that. And I prayed about it. Dear Lord, I just can't take it any longer. I just can't take it any longer. Show me the way to get out. So a woman came in to teach the school, a girl, I should say, but she was a woman grown. And she said, well, I worked for my board and went to Lewiston Normal. And I didn't have anywhere near the start that you've had of going to school and relearning stuff. I'd gone back to school and I had a teacher that was a wonderful teacher and he'd given me certain courses, really. I caged in on some of them afterward by reviewing them in a real class and taking an examination in a rhetoric, for example, which was really grammar. And I was good in it. Was this Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mushletts?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yes. So you were rather well prepared to go down. Oh, in a way. And in a way I didn't know anything. And I had nothing. And I dressed like a ragamuffin from the streets of New York, like the Arab. But anyway, I made up my mind, and Stella Harlan, she was Stella Buckby, was her name. She told me she worked for the board.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I said, how did you find out about it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And she said, well, she told me some friend that had worked for her board and went to Lewiston. She went right down to the Dean of Women and see if she could get a job working for someone for her board, board and room, of course. And she did, and she got a place. And these people were very nice to her and she stayed with them for three years. And she went all five years. She hadn't been to high school or anything. And she all went all five years to Lewiston. At that time, you could do that the normal way. And they had what was known as the academic department, which was high school, the first three years, and then two years of professional. That's the way they divided it. They called it academic and professional. And the professional was, there were two years of that. And when you graduated, you had a life diploma. Well, eventually, I graduated, but I didn't do like Stella. She went down and just stayed there the five years. And then in the summertime, she had already, she lived up on Cedar Creek, and there was a family of girls living on Cedar Creek. And they had come up there, that had come up there from Juliana. And every summer, they went down on the river to pack fruit. And there was good money in packing fruit. And she went down on the river to pack fruit. But my mother, she went up in the air. I wasn't to be allowed to go down on the river to pack fruit or go away. My goodness, nothing but tuffs down there. That had just ruined me. I'd come back. I just wouldn't be anything. And she didn't consider how I'd come be what I was and how I'd made it my mind to go to school and had that all in mind. My mother never took my measure. She never did. And therein lay on happiness for both her and for me. She could have been much happier if she'd have had a different attitude. But then that's neither here nor there either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Stella, was Stella Harlan the person that got you on to going to the university?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, she was the one that told me that it could be done. And she presented away. And I asked who the Dean of Women was because I didn't know. And she told me what her name was. So I wrote to the Dean of Women and asked her to leave it was possible. And told her that it had been my lifelong dream that I'd get to come. And this woman had told me that she had worked for her board.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And was it possible to get a place and that I would like to come to Lewiston and go to school?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, I got a lovely letter back from her, the Dean of Women. She became a very good friend of mine, Anna Webster Lidle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was her name? Lidle?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: L -I -T -L -E, Lidle. Anna Webster Lidle. She always called the sign the full name. She lived with her parents. She was what most country people would have called an old maid, I guess. But she was a Spencer lady. But a very good looking woman. She was a regal looking woman. She had a queenly bearing. And looked regal and very good looking. But she wasn't a young woman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she say it was indeed possible?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: She wrote me and said it was and that she would, yes. And she looked up a place for me and she thought I'd like. It was a place that was a very warm hearted woman and although there were children there, they were girls and she thought it would be a nice place for me. It was a Stacey's. So I went to her stuff. Oh, yes. And she told me, Contact her again and tell her if I expected to come. And she would make the arrangements with Mrs. Stacey. And if I could come down a couple of days early, they'd get acquainted with the family. It would be good. But I got some work of some kind. I don't know what it was. Anyway, I took every bit of work that I could get. I guess I'm doing something of a dollar a day. Almost picking food down at the food ranch down below our place. And it lasted a little longer. And I helped cook for the Thrashers in another place. And I got paid a dollar a day for that. Oh, that was a fortune. That's where I got the rest of the fifty dollars. Now I had to have shoes and I had to have a few things. Well, at last I embarked.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I thought I had.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And I remember that Raymond Heron was going to the Stella Buckweedy. And Raymond was my hero. He had been to Lewiston -Normal and had played ball down there. And the coach were very good friends. And I liked Raymond. He was a neighbor boy, you know. He played basketball on the Lewiston -Normal team and had his picture in the paper. And I just thought the world of Raymond. Of course, he had been growing up for a little while, you know. I was a little girl. I think Stella always resented me a little. I realized that she did when I taught school out at American age. Because Raymond and I were such awful good friends that we always were talking about so many things. Nothing in the world, goodness sakes a life. I had always been the little girl from next door. And he had always been the hero, the neighborhood hero, to me. So it was nothing in the world but between us other than that. But she just resented me a little. Because until he died, we were friends. Good friends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what happened? He went down with you?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, no. He didn t intend to go down with me. That was accidental entirely. But he got on the same train that I went down to Lewiston on. He was on the train going down to see her. And she had taught school at Myrtle that year. She wasn t teaching up at her school. And so he got up there. So he came and sat with me. And he told me how to do, not to let this bother me, not to let that bother me. And he said, You know, you know things. And he said, I m not a bit afraid after you get started down there that you won t get along. He said, You won t get along because you know that you know things. So you just simply, okay, he told me a lot of things about how I should get along. And remember, there would be a lot of moving for me. They knew I would come along. He said, You ll get along so many things first. Don t you let it get you down. He said, and that was his family s. They were traditionally pretty smart people, but they all started the school and all came home, all but Raymond. All the rest of them did. They gave up and came home. They just didn t have to stay with them at home. But they had a nice home and a good place to go.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And so he shook hands with me and said, Good luck to me. I felt pretty lonesome when he left when he got off the train. But I went down all by myself. And when I got there, I was met by a couple of girls who belonged to a new YWCA that Miss Lidle, the Indian women, had organized, got the older girls in. And they were out to meet the new incoming students. So they walked up to Stacy s with me, carried my suitcase for me. I had an awful lot of my suitcase. But I remember my uncle had given me a suitcase that didn t look too terribly bad. I loved her too. She was. That was a help. But I didn t have much energy. And I was awful lonesome there for a while. Mrs. Stacy was a musician, and she had some musical friends over that night. They had a kind of a musical downstairs. She said, You probably want to go up to your room and unpack and set out things. You probably want to, she said. But you ll have the friends downstairs. Well, her kids stayed downstairs. And I went upstairs. I felt isolated and alone. But I made out. And we all got to be very good friends. But her mother lived over in Clarkson when she got sick and died. And Stacy had to be away from home a lot. So when I was home Thanksgiving vacation, she decided she d have to have a girl, a regular girl. And when I got back down after being home for vacation. And by the way, the folks got anxious enough to see me. I said, I can t come home. I can t spend my money. Well, they sent me trains there to come home on. To the penny. To the penny. I got money to come home on and to go back on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was Thanksgiving vacation?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yeah. Mrs. Carlson, I feel a little.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: You had to make a make your room and board while you were going to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that difficult?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: No, I had lovely people. Stacy was a lovely person. And I worked with her and the girls were nice. And she did a lot of things for me. She helped me and she saw that I hadn t been out a great deal. And she realized that I was an awkward country girl. But she also took into consideration. I wouldn t like to have this advertised either. But actually, she did take into consideration that I was a sensitive girl. And that I was worth doing something about. And so she was very nice to me. Very nice to me. And she s naturally nice. And I got along very nicely. But after her mother got sick, as I said, then she decided she had to have a girl for all her years. She got me another place. And I stayed with a friend of hers then for a little while. That is, I mean, who needed a girl for a short time. And then after, at Christmas time, or at the break in the semester, I went to another place. And that was a terrible place to be. I just lived and existed and did my work as well as I could and got along and waited for the year to be over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the difference between that place and the other place?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: The difference was why. The difference was people. And they were good people. She was a good person. Of course, some people are born to be nice. We used to have an expression that they were born ladies and other people were not. But that isn t the distinction. But some people, by birth, know how to get along with other people. And some people don t. And they are critical and selfish and that sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there a lot of demands made on you at this place to work you harder?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, I had harder work to do, but the work was harder because there was less good feeling. And no matter how well I tried to do the thing, there was no appreciation shown. But of course there was plenty of fault finding. But then to tell anybody what the difference is, it s very hard to do. Especially if you re not wanting to really blacken anybody s character. Well, you wouldn t use any names. No. But I was just curious. But it didn t work out so well. It sounds like there weren t considerate of you in the same way. I was very glad when the year was over. Although I must say that by the time the year was over, I was getting along better. And I think she genuinely, he didn t see me go really. I was beginning to do the work she wanted done, I suppose maybe. There were two sides to it. I suppose I was doing it more the way she wanted it done, and she liked it better. Maybe that was the reason. But I believe she actually genuinely started to see me go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the work that you were expected to do for your room and board?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, get up and get breakfast at that place I got up and went down in the basement. Started the fire going, stirred up the fire and got the fire going. Went upstairs and got the breakfast, that is, started the cereal cooking. And usually I set the breakfast table and got things all ready. But they hadn t gotten up to eat yet. I usually went to school before they did. But sometimes he got up to eat, and I had to have his breakfast on the table. Toast and his egg and so on. They were particular about how it was cooked, which was all right. I learned to be particular about things. And then I got started on the days, whatever it was. If the washing had been done the day before, I saw to it that, well, I did that at night. I rolled it up for the ironing, but got it all ready for the washer wall. But if she was going to iron, she had come early, and she was usually there. And she was an awful nice old lady, and she felt sorry for me, and she d say, I ll take care of that. I ll take care of that. She was always saying that to me. But I didn t wait much for her to do.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: If there was anything to, if there was any mess made or anything, I had to clean that up.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And I had the fire going, and then they had a fire in the upstairs, a stove, I mean. And when I went up to dress and get ready, I was supposed to, but they had Kimling, and Mr. Ward laid that. And I was going to do that the night before. He always did that, always took that and something. And then I had to start a little fire in that stove so it would be warm when the children came up. They weren t out yet, when I went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much work to do when you got home from school?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, Lord, yes, I had different things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you supposed to make supper and do the dishes and all that business?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yes, all the dishes, all the dishes. Usually, dishes were left from breakfast. They might be left from breakfast. She sometimes washed them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you have much time to study?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, after dinner was over, when I had the evening free, and of course, I babysat sometimes, she always put the kids to bed. They went to bed early, and she put the kids to bed. And I watched them, of course. And only once in a while did they bother me. Once in a while, something would matter to them, and I d have to go and see to them. Their father would be there many times, and he d do the looking after. I never went into the room.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: You said just a little bit ago that you were an awkward country girl when you first came to town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you think about it, what was it that you lacked, or what was it that you were, that made you an awkward country? How did that work in it, to be in town?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, I think that it was mainly that I didn t know how to get along with those people. I d been around people, and I understood that people out in the country, they were more or less like me, and I didn t feel that they were any different. But when I went down to Lewiston, and among the people who had girls working for their board, they were people who I felt that they were the difference. Now, I don t know that they did. But I think that probably the difference was in me that I felt this, that they were so different, and they were looking upon me that way. So I felt very, I was self -conscious.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the difference that you perceived? What did you think the difference was, between town people and country people?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, they were...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they more sophisticated?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, yes, of course they were. They are, yet. That is, that class of people. Not the ordinary class of people. But you take the so -called elite. They adopt a little more sophisticated heirs. Of course, after you get to knowing them, and you get onto their little ways of doing things, it doesn t terrify you anymore because you see through them, and you know what they are. And then you can be like Kipling. You can walk with kings and or lose your virtue. Or you can walk with clouds and or lose your virtue, or talk with kings and or lose the common touch. But you have to be above it yourself, and you have to lift out of that, and you don t come out of the country bean field, anything in the cornfield, or anything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what, right?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: At first, you have to live through it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were some of the little, the little, the heirs or the ways that the elite had in the towns, in the most of them?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, they had different, they had better manners. They had manners of eating. They had more formal meals. More sophistication about it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, there was style. It was kept up. And they spent a lot more time keeping their houses up. What was spent out in the country, the house got torn up. It didn t go like I let mine go here. But not so. And they had to be kept up wisely all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they spend money on appearances? The town people? On clothing and?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, they had better clothes, of course. And they dressed up for you, usually. In some places they dressed for dinner. In the evening, they called it dinner, not in the farm. They called it suffering, and ate the same old clothes you had on all day, and all that stuff. Oh, there s was a vast difference. You know there s a vast difference. You certainly heard that from a lot of other people. What I m curious about is the difference between town and country back then, you know, in the early years of the century. There was a big difference. And just what the supposed difference in status was, I mean I ve heard a couple of times that city people were, especially those that had some money, thought of themselves as elite, were really, some of them were very much showy, you know. And I don t know, I mean I ve heard different things about it. Well, I think some were. But I think for the most part, the difference existed in the mind of me and of people from the country. It wasn t very long. Now if I went to knowing what I do now, say, or what I learned in five years time after I had been there, when I went to Lewiston, no, I did go down. And I worked for my board one year, when I was after I had taught. I just thought I d save the money. I didn t have much money, and I was going to go to summer school, and I thought I needed more money at summer school. I get such very, very wages in. And I thought I d save the money, and so I asked for a place for the last quarter. And I didn t feel as I did the first time when I went in. And I went in and said that I had come to work for my board. And she was surprised that I had taught school, and she talked with me very nicely. And she introduced me to her husband on an equal footing. When we were equal, I never felt anything else. I never did feel anything else but that we were equals. And when I went back to Lewiston, I certainly didn t want to work for my board because I wanted to have the time. She wanted me to come and stay at her house. She said, You don t have to do anything. You don t have to do anything to your board. I ve got a girl doing that. But come on over and we can visit, and we can do something else. And I went over and sold evenings for an hour or two for the boys. She was at the close meetup. And I sold, or after her girl was off for an evening, I d only say to her, You go out. I m not going out tonight. I ll keep the boys, and so on. And we were just very friendly. But that was afterward. Now, if I don t have so, you know, you know it s like in Shakespeare. Who knows everything. You either go to the Bible or Shakespeare and you find it all knowledgeable people. That s the kind of people I like. And I always wanted to be with a knowledgeable person. One of the new things. Because if you know things, you understand things. You know how people feel. You can put them to ease. I liked, I enjoyed being that kind of person. And I don t want to see people feeling out of place. And they don t with me. I see to it that they don t. I know it s aiming people who are feeling awkward and not at home. And I m immediately spot him and immediately do something about it. And in two or three days he s like all the others. It doesn t take long. But if the learning to do has to come through, all of it has to come through experience, and that experience has to come to the newcomer himself. It takes a much longer time. And there s much more pain involved in the operation. And that s what happened to you and Louis. And it took you learning it yourself. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think it was that you learned? That those people were no different than you?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh no. I didn t learn that so much. They worked so much different. But I learned, of course, that being environment, I d learned I d read about environment versus heredity and that stuff. And I knew that heredity counted for a lot, and certainly was not to be discounted. That environment gave us different backgrounds. And that from different backgrounds we had misunderstandings. I soon learned. I learned rapidly. I was a rapid learner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Louis when you were first down there, was it a very big place to you? Was it really out of the kind of?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: It wasn t really awfully big. It was bigger than anything I had been used to. I was kept on the farm most of the time. And I only went to Troy once in a while. Troy seemed like quite a little town to me. And to come up to Moscow, I remember I came up to Moscow to see Roosevelt when he spoke here in 1909. Well, he was in 1909. Goodness, I would ve been during that year. He was here in February, but I would ve been 13.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yes, I would ve been 13 that year. I didn t think I was that old. But I hadn t been out to see anything, and I remember that I went with Mr. Helm over to see him. I was one of our neighbors on home. And we stood up there, and that is Mr. Helm and I, we were more adventurous than the rest of our crowd. And we pushed our way after the ropes that went around as a fence around the wheat pile that he stood on. I was a little tired. I was, I still had an awful lot of gawkingness, I know that. And I wasn t sure of myself. It took me a long time to be sure of myself. And then they had trained their children. They talked better. They used better English. But of course now you can t tell a difference. To talk lazily and slovenly seems to be the ideal. And they all talk that way. There isn t a difference now that there used to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to you when you were in Lewiston? Did you change a great deal?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, of course I did. I learned. I learned a lot of things. Of course I changed. I m a different person than what I was when I was a child. In some ways I understand people and I ve lost some of my naivety. A lot of it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And I suppose I ve lost some other things. Well, you know, what I was thinking of was the experience of being away from home and going to school and getting teacher training. Oh, sure I got through that. That came along right away. By the end of the year I was well a climate to do that sort of thing. But I went home. I fully intended to go back to Lewiston -Narmel the second year. And of course I had in mind going to teaching and that s what they talked to me at home and friends talked to me and take this rural school program and teach in a rural school. And you could go down there and go the one year and then go back and go another year and get a certificate and teach. But that would be a short course and I d get to teaching. Just get me eighteen and I could teach. That was my ideal. Well, they talked to me different down there. The teachers did. Oh, don t let yourself do that. First thing you know you ll get bogged down. Maybe you ll marry some farmer or kid, some kid farmer, one of the teachers I know said to me. And that ll be the end of you. Don t ever do that. Set your sights higher. Well, I did but I knew it was kind of impossible and I hated awful bad to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they want you to set your sights for? What was the higher?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, they wanted a higher education and a better education before I went to work and they were right. Dead right. Nobody should go out and teach on that. Thank God they did not allow me to do it and that I didn t get to on that short course. Those people that went out and taught, the children that they taught were only half taught. And I know now with what I do know about teaching. And with some of the things that I have been privileged to accomplish in my teaching, I know that I was not a teacher at that time. I hadn t become that yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you had your high school?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Of course not. I went from an eighth grade in a school, in a country school. The longest school term that I had was the last one and that one was we had seven months of school. And that was the first one. Otherwise, five and six month terms. Oh, certainly it was a good thing and my sights should be higher than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what happened? Did you go back?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I didn t go back to Lewiston. The folks were thinking about going to Michigan to visit my other people and I thought it would be wonderful for them. I passed the eighth grade the year that I was 12 in March and I passed in February. So I passed the eighth grade. But I went back to that country school. And I had gone to that last seven month term of school. The next one was the sixth month. And I think for two or three years they had six month terms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this on American Ridge?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: That s on American Ridge. That s on American Ridge. It s been torn down a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn t you go to school in Troy also at some point?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I just got getting around to that. That was the year that my folks wanted to go to school. That was my second year going to school. And I had already learned that I could get high school subjects in any high school. And that s what I got in the academic at Lewiston. Except that they didn t have any place for me to take another subject and they d let me have the first year I was down there. I told them I was going to take this rural short course. So they had sort of had that in mind when they made up my curriculum. And I was kind of fouled up a little bit there. And I got a professional health and physiology course. Health and sanitation they called it. Well, goodness, right up my alley. And I d had that a couple or three times going back to school. And I just loved it. And I was a little shy so I could get up and trace the blood without ever having to learn it. I was a wonder there among some of these professional girls. They d never learned it when they went to school and they thought it was terrible to get in and learn it. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did I know it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And they just kept wanting my notebook and everything else. Funny. And as I say, I would have had an awful time with the normal. But I did know and this going back to school had given me leverage so that I could get along because of it. And some of these girls that were very snappy and certainly up to date, very stylish, they said, I don t see how you do it. I don t see how you do it, say to me. And they wouldn t snub me because I knew things. So I might say that my brain s helped me a little, you know. And funny enough, I struck up some very peculiar friendships there among people that wouldn t have seen me otherwise. I couldn t have seen me enough. But not in my inconspicuous outfits or conspicuous for the wrong reasons. So I went to Troy. I thought as I say, I d delved around and found out I could do that. So I said, the folks, you go on and I ll stand, Troy. And then I can t go back and forth, but I can come home on the weekends. By that time the roads were getting better and I figured I could come out on the weekend. They could come and get me. My brother was old enough that he could come in with the horses and I could ride home if necessary. Come out on Friday night and stay till Sunday night and help the kids. And I was making a little more money packing fruit and cooking for harvest ants and things of that sort. I didn t work anymore for weekly wages. And I got a job at the telephone office then as assistant in the telephone office. And that gave me a little monthly wage. And I was so far ahead of any of the high school kids, and Troy, even though I d only had a freshman year, I d had a little more than a freshman year at the Luster -Dermel because they allowed you to make high school in four years. And as I say, I d had that one professional subject the first part of the year and I had a second one. I forgot who that was, the second half of the year. And I knew how to make capital of it. And I had this rhetoric, but it just hadn t come to me that I was to make it all come into my life and use it. And Miss Lytle was a masterful English teacher. When she would come into the classes and lecture occasionally, she didn t teach them, but she lectured occasionally, and showed how they did. And I took everything to heart, and I took Latin one year Latin, and I made that year of Latin pay. And I learned a lot. And I learned a lot more than what I even could get credit for. And I also took German, a half year of German, but it was the last half. And by taking the last half, when it came down to it, I wanted to sign up for a course in German then, and they let me take the second year. And they said, Well, goodness gracious, if you took the second year of German and passed in it, it means you knew the first year. Well, I said, I only know what I went over. But I went over to myself, well, you must have done a good job. Well, I said, I read it. So they gave me credit, and I got credit there for a half year I never took.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened? How long did you stay at Troy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: A year and a half. I finished in a year and a half.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You finished high school?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I finished high school. A year and a half.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then what did you do?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, I went to summer school and took it and got a state certificate. I got a second grade certificate, but it was written on it. It was good for one year, because until I taught a year to get one. Well, the next year, I took a country school and it was out in six months, seven months. I had a seven -month term in school. They were all about seven months that year. But I got a seven -month term instead of getting an eighth, because I wanted to go the last quarter to normal. I went down to normal and got the professional work for the last quarter. And I built them into letting me take an extra subject and stayed on for summer school. That gave me two parts. I'd been to summer school a year before. That gave me three -quarters. And I took night school and got a credit or two. And I had plenty of credits for one year's work, so I applied for a normal school certificate, second grade. Well, my state certificate for second grade was good. I taught one year and sent in my recommendations from the school board. We're glad to give me recommendations. I thought I was a wonderful teacher, because I wasn't in a Norwegian neighborhood. I loved the kids and they loved me. They taught me Norwegian, and I taught them lots of things. And they gave me a good recommendation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This year of teaching now, this one year of teaching that you did in between, when did this come? After you had been at Troy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: After I had graduated high school. I didn't do that until I got a certificate. I got into my head that I had to do it the right way, and I did it the right way. I went to high school, finished it, and went to summer school. Got you, too. And took the examination, the state examination, for a state certificate, not a normal school certificate. And then by the next spring, I'd already completed my three units of... No, I hadn't completed the third unit by the spring. But by the time summer was over, I completed the three units. So I got my state certificate, made good, for a second grade state certificate. And they told me if I went up and took an examination in physical geography, rhetoric,

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: and... If I got to take those three... an examination in those three subjects, that I could be in line for a first grade certificate. Well, I had no shit to do it, but I'd have had to take no little time to review them in school, because I didn't know just exactly what they want for pedagogy. But I'd sent in my requirements that I had for it, and for taking the exam, so I could have taken the exam. I was to be admitted for it. But then in the meantime, I qualified for a first grade normal certificate. But at the end of the first quarter, they looked at my record. Well, I'd taught a year, and got my credits for it, because my school board had sent in such a glowing account. Rest their hearts. They weren't asking to do it either.
